title: Best Stand Mixer Attachments in 2026: Expand What Your KitchenAid Can Do
description: Already own a stand mixer? The best stand mixer attachments can turn it into a pasta maker, meat grinder, ice cream machine, and more. Here's exactly what's worth buying in 2026.

sections.heading: Why Attachments Matter More Than a New Mixer
sections.body: The best stand mixer attachments are the single most cost-effective upgrade you can make to a kitchen you already have. If you own a KitchenAid, a Cuisinart, or an Ankarsrum, you are sitting on a powerful motor hub that most people use for exactly two things: whipping cream and making cookie dough. That is a waste. The power head on a KitchenAid Artisan, for example, is engineered to drive far more than a wire whip. Pasta rollers, meat grinders, food processors, ice cream makers, grain mills, and spiralizers all plug directly into the same power take-off hub. Buying a dedicated pasta machine costs you counter space, storage space, and another power cord. Buying the pasta roller attachment costs you none of those things and uses the motor you already paid for. The math is straightforward. A full suite of quality attachments typically runs less than the price of a second mid-range appliance, and they all store together in a single drawer or cabinet. For anyone who cooks seriously and wants to reduce appliance clutter, attachments are not optional accessories — they are the entire point of owning a stand mixer in the first place.

sections.heading: The 7 Best Stand Mixer Attachments Tested and Ranked
sections.body:
These seven attachment categories deliver the most real-world value. They are ranked by how dramatically they expand your mixer's utility relative to their cost and how often a home cook will actually reach for them.

Pasta Roller and Cutter Set. This is the attachment that converts the most skeptics. Fresh pasta made with a roller attachment is genuinely different from dried pasta — thinner, silkier, and faster to cook. The KitchenAid pasta roller set typically includes a roller, a spaghetti cutter, and a fettuccine cutter. The roller feeds dough hands-free while you guide it, which is a significant ergonomic improvement over hand-cranked machines. The quality of the output is excellent at every thickness setting.

Meat Grinder. The stand mixer meat grinder attachment turns your machine into a full-service butcher tool. You control the fat ratio, the grind coarseness, and the freshness. For anyone making burgers, meatballs, sausage, or pâté, this is transformative. Most grinder attachments include both fine and coarse grinding plates. The food tray is large enough to handle a meaningful batch in one session.

Food Grinder and Fruit and Vegetable Strainer. This combination attachment handles grinding, pureeing, and straining in one unit. It is the right tool for making tomato sauce from scratch, apple butter, or baby food. The strainer cone separates seeds and skins automatically, which saves significant prep time.

Ice Cream Maker Bowl. The freeze-the-bowl style ice cream attachment is one of the cleverest uses of the mixer's planetary action. You freeze the bowl overnight, add your base, and the dasher churns it to soft-serve consistency in about 20 to 30 minutes. The results are genuinely good — smooth, creamy, and customizable. The main limitation is that the bowl needs 15 or more hours in the freezer before use, so spontaneous ice cream is not on the menu.

Spiral Dough Hook (Upgrade). The standard C-hook that ships with most KitchenAid mixers is functional but not ideal for stiff, high-hydration doughs. The spiral dough hook — sold as an aftermarket upgrade by KitchenAid and third-party brands — engages the dough more efficiently, reduces strain on the motor, and produces better gluten development. If you bake bread regularly, this is one of the cheapest and highest-impact upgrades available.

Grain Mill. The grain mill attachment grinds whole wheat berries, corn, oats, and other grains into fresh flour. It is a niche purchase, but for serious bread bakers or anyone managing dietary restrictions, fresh-milled flour is a meaningful quality step up. The output fineness is adjustable.

Spiralizer with Peel, Core, and Slice. The spiralizer attachment handles zucchini noodles, apple spirals, potato ribbons, and more. It is faster and more consistent than hand-held spiralizers and produces longer, more uniform strands. It is not an essential purchase for every cook, but for households that eat low-carb or cook a lot of vegetable-forward dishes, it earns its place.

sections.heading: Which Attachments Are Worth the Price vs. Overrated
sections.body:
Not every attachment justifies its price tag. Here is an honest breakdown of what delivers and what disappoints.

Worth every dollar: The pasta roller and cutter set is the gold standard. It is expensive but it replaces a dedicated appliance and produces restaurant-quality results. The meat grinder is similarly high-value — the cost is modest, the utility is enormous, and the quality of home-ground meat is noticeably better than pre-ground supermarket product. The spiral dough hook upgrade is almost embarrassingly good value given how cheap it is relative to the improvement it delivers for bread bakers.

Solid but situational: The ice cream maker bowl is excellent if you make frozen desserts more than a few times per year. If you make it once a summer, the math gets harder to justify. The grain mill is outstanding for its target user — someone who bakes with whole grains regularly — but it is a specialty tool that most kitchens do not need.

Overrated or redundant: The slicer and shredder attachment gets mixed reviews. The blade quality on the official KitchenAid version is adequate but not exceptional, and a good mandoline or box grater handles most of the same tasks faster for less money. The juicer attachment is similarly underwhelming — dedicated citrus juicers are cheaper and easier to clean. The pasta press (extruder) attachment produces shapes like rigatoni and fusilli but requires much stiffer dough than the roller, and the output quality is inconsistent unless you dial in the recipe carefully. It is worth trying if you already own the roller set, but it should not be your first pasta attachment purchase.

sections.heading: Compatibility Guide: KitchenAid vs. Cuisinart vs. Ankarsrum
sections.body:
Compatibility is the most important thing to check before buying any attachment, and it is also the most confusing part of the shopping process.

KitchenAid is the dominant platform. The vast majority of stand mixer attachments on the market are designed for KitchenAid's universal power hub, which has remained consistent across the Artisan, Classic, Professional 5 Plus, and Professional 600 lines for decades. If you own any of these models, you have access to the full KitchenAid attachment ecosystem plus a large number of compatible third-party options. The one exception is the KitchenAid Mini — its power hub is the same design, but the smaller footprint means some attachments, particularly the pasta roller set, can be awkward to use because the mixer sits lower and the clearance between the attachment and the bowl is reduced.

Cuisinart stand mixers use a different hub design. Cuisinart makes its own attachment line, but the selection is significantly smaller than KitchenAid's. The pasta roller, meat grinder, and ice cream bowl are available, but niche attachments like the grain mill are not. Third-party compatibility is also more limited. If attachment versatility is a priority, this is a meaningful reason to choose KitchenAid over Cuisinart when buying a mixer.

Ankarsrum is a Swedish machine with a fundamentally different design philosophy. It uses a rotating bowl rather than a planetary head, and its attachment system is entirely proprietary. The Ankarsrum attachment ecosystem is well-made but small. It excels at bread dough and has a dedicated following among serious bakers, but if you want the full range of attachments — pasta, meat grinding, ice cream — KitchenAid remains the more versatile platform.

Third-party attachments: Several brands produce KitchenAid-compatible attachments at lower price points than the official KitchenAid versions. Quality varies significantly. For the meat grinder and pasta roller in particular, the official KitchenAid attachments are worth the premium because the tolerances and material quality are noticeably better. For the spiral dough hook, third-party options are generally fine. Always verify the specific model compatibility before purchasing any third-party attachment.

sections.heading: How to Buy: Key Features to Check Before You Order
sections.body:
Before you add any attachment to your cart, run through this checklist.

Hub compatibility first. Confirm your mixer model and the attachment's stated compatibility. Do not assume — KitchenAid's own product pages list compatible models explicitly. The power hub connection is the same across most full-size KitchenAid models, but always verify.

Material quality. For anything that contacts food under stress — meat grinder plates, pasta roller drums, grain mill burrs — look for stainless steel or food-grade metal construction. Plastic components in high-stress positions wear out and can contaminate food. The official KitchenAid pasta roller, for example, uses chrome-plated steel rollers that hold their surface finish over years of use.

Cleaning requirements. Some attachments are dishwasher safe; others are not. The pasta roller is hand-wash only — water and soap damage the internal rollers. The meat grinder parts are generally dishwasher safe. Know what you are committing to before you buy, because an attachment you hate cleaning is an attachment you stop using.

Storage footprint. Attachments take up space. The pasta roller set, for instance, includes three separate pieces. The spiralizer comes with multiple blade cones. Think about where these will live before you buy. A dedicated drawer or a cabinet shelf with a storage case makes a significant difference in whether you actually reach for them regularly.

Bundled accessories vs. base attachment. Many attachments are sold in multiple configurations. The pasta roller is available as a roller-only, a two-cutter set, or a larger bundle with additional cutter shapes. Buy the bundle if you know you will use the additional cutters — the per-piece price is better. If you are not sure, start with the base roller and add cutters later.

Warranty and return policy. KitchenAid's official attachments carry a one-year warranty. Third-party attachments vary. For higher-priced attachments like the pasta roller or grain mill, the warranty matters — these are precision tools and manufacturing defects do occur.

sections.heading: Final Verdict and Buying Recommendations
sections.body:
Here is the direct answer to who should buy what.

If you cook pasta more than twice a month, buy the pasta roller and cutter set first. It is the highest-impact attachment available and the one most likely to change how you cook on a weekly basis. It is not cheap, but it replaces a dedicated appliance and produces results that are genuinely better than what you get from a hand-cranked machine.

If you make burgers, meatballs, or sausage at home, add the meat grinder. It is modestly priced, easy to use, and the quality improvement over pre-ground supermarket meat is real and immediate. This is the attachment with the best value-to-cost ratio in the entire lineup.

If you bake bread at least once a week, upgrade to the spiral dough hook. It is inexpensive, takes two minutes to swap in, and makes a measurable difference in dough quality and motor longevity.

If you make frozen desserts regularly, the ice cream maker bowl is excellent. Just plan ahead — the bowl needs overnight freezing. If you are an occasional ice cream maker, it is harder to justify.

If you are a serious whole-grain baker, the grain mill is a specialty purchase that delivers exceptional results for its target user. Everyone else can skip it.

Skip the slicer and shredder and the juicer unless you have a specific need that your current tools cannot meet. They are not bad attachments, but they are not better than the dedicated tools they replace.

For anyone visiting our full kitchen gear guides at /best/kitchen or browsing by category at /category/kitchen, the broader context for these recommendations is that a well-equipped stand mixer with three or four quality attachments genuinely replaces five to seven single-purpose appliances. That is a compelling case for investing in attachments rather than expanding your appliance collection.

faq.question: Are KitchenAid attachments compatible with all KitchenAid mixer models?
faq.answer: Most KitchenAid attachments are compatible with all full-size KitchenAid tilt-head and bowl-lift models, including the Artisan, Classic, Professional 5 Plus, and Professional 600. The power hub design has remained consistent across these models for decades. The KitchenAid Mini uses the same hub connection but has a smaller footprint that can make some attachments — particularly the pasta roller set — more awkward to use due to reduced clearance. Always check the compatibility list on the product page for your specific model before purchasing.

faq.question: Can I use KitchenAid attachments on a Cuisinart or other brand stand mixer?
faq.answer: No. KitchenAid attachments are designed specifically for KitchenAid's power hub and will not fit Cuisinart, Ankarsrum, Kenwood, or other brand mixers. Each manufacturer uses a proprietary hub design. Cuisinart makes its own attachment line for its stand mixers, but the selection is considerably smaller than KitchenAid's ecosystem. If attachment versatility is important to you, this is a meaningful factor to consider when choosing which mixer brand to invest in.

faq.question: Is the KitchenAid pasta roller attachment worth the price?
faq.answer: Yes, for anyone who makes fresh pasta more than occasionally. The pasta roller attachment produces excellent results — thin, even sheets that are difficult to achieve consistently with a hand-cranked machine — and it does so hands-free, which is a real ergonomic advantage. It replaces a dedicated pasta machine that would cost similar money and take up counter and storage space. The main caveat is that it is hand-wash only, so you need to be comfortable with that cleaning requirement before buying.

faq.question: How do I clean stand mixer attachments properly?
faq.answer: Cleaning requirements vary by attachment. The meat grinder plates and housing are generally dishwasher safe, though hand washing extends their life. The pasta roller is strictly hand-wash only — water and soap can damage the internal steel rollers and cause rust or surface degradation. The ice cream maker bowl should be hand washed and thoroughly dried before freezing. The grain mill components should be wiped clean rather than washed, as moisture can damage the burrs. Always check the care instructions included with each specific attachment before the first use.

faq.question: What is the best first attachment to buy for a KitchenAid stand mixer?
faq.answer: For most home cooks, the pasta roller and cutter set is the best first attachment purchase because it delivers the most dramatic expansion of what your mixer can do and produces results that are noticeably better than alternatives. However, if you cook a lot of ground meat dishes — burgers, meatballs, sausage — the meat grinder attachment offers better value per dollar and is the smarter first buy. For bread bakers specifically, upgrading to a spiral dough hook is the cheapest and most immediately impactful improvement you can make.

faq.question: Do third-party KitchenAid-compatible attachments work as well as the official ones?
faq.answer: It depends on the attachment. For the spiral dough hook, third-party options are generally comparable to the official KitchenAid version and can save you money. For precision attachments like the pasta roller and meat grinder, the official KitchenAid versions are worth the premium — the tolerances, material quality, and surface finishes are noticeably better, and these are the attachments where manufacturing quality directly affects the food you produce. Always verify that any third-party attachment explicitly lists your specific mixer model as compatible before purchasing.
